exercises.name: Lever Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Step 2: Adjust the seat and pivot so the lever handles start just above and behind your head when your arms are extended; select an appropriate weight.
Step 3: Reach up and grasp the handles with a comfortable (typically neutral or slightly pronated) grip, keeping a small, fixed bend in the elbows.
Step 4: Begin with the shoulders stretched overhead and the chest lifted slightly, maintaining a stable torso against the pad.
Step 5: Exhale and pull the lever in a controlled arc down and back toward your hips/upper thighs, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the lats.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement with elbows by your sides and a full lat contraction; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly return the lever to the start position, resisting the weight and keeping tension on the lats without letting the shoulders fully relax.
Step 8: Repeat for desired reps; maintain strict form—no torso rocking, keep core engaged, and avoid locking the elbows at the end ranges.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover that moves the arms in an overhead-to-hip arc to lengthen and contract the lats while also engaging the pectorals and serratus anterior. Performed on a lever machine with the torso supported by a backrest for stability.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down.
Sit with your chest pressed into the pad, grip the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, and keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement.
Start with the arms hanging at your sides (shoulders relaxed) and brace your core to prevent movement of the torso.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (about 90 degrees), keeping the elbows slightly bent and the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the top while avoiding shoulder shrugging (do not lift with the neck or upper trapezius).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position under control, maintaining chest contact with the pad and minimizing momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form without swinging.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported lateral raise performed on a lever machine to isolate the lateral deltoids; the chest pad prevents torso swing so the delts do the work. Arms are raised out to the sides with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging.
Stand upright beside the lever with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; grasp the handle with the working hand (thumb wrapped) at your side.
Keep a slight bend in the elbow and the wrist neutral; maintain an upright torso with shoulders down and back (no leaning or twisting).
Exhale and lift the arm straight out to the side in a smooth arc until the upper arm reaches roughly shoulder height (parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the scapula stable and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position under control, resisting any momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides if performing single-arm sets.
exercises.description: A standing lateral raise performed on a lever/cable station that isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting the arm out to the side from the body. The movement uses a controlled upward arc to shoulder height and a slow descent to emphasize the delts.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit at your sides roughly at hip level when seated; select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit at your sides roughly at hip level when seated; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and grasp the handles with a neutral grip (palms facing inward).
Keep a slight bend in the elbows and the forearms relatively fixed; start with the arms hanging at your sides.
Exhale and raise your upper arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your elbows reach shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep your torso still and avoid leaning or using momentum; do not excessively shrug the shoulders.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position with controlled movement.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining form throughout.
exercises.description: Seated machine lever lateral raise isolates the lateral delts by abducting the arms out to the sides against resistance, emphasizing controlled movement and minimal torso motion. It targets shoulder width and middle deltoid development.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean grip Front Squat
exercises.notes: Take a deep breath, brace your core, and keep your chest up with an upright torso throughout the movement. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, unrack the barbell into a front-rack position with elbows high and the bar resting on your front delts/clavicles.
Take a deep breath, brace your core, and keep your chest up with an upright torso throughout the movement.
Descend by bending at the hips and knees simultaneously, keeping knees tracking over toes until thighs reach parallel or slightly below.
Drive through your full foot to stand back up, maintaining high elbows and core tension to prevent the bar from rolling forward.
Complete all reps with control, then carefully re-rack the barbell or lower it safely to the floor.
exercises.description: A barbell front squat performed from a clean (front-rack) grip where the bar rests on the front delts/upper chest; you squat down to roughly parallel and stand back up. This variation emphasizes knee extension, placing primary load on the quads while the upper back and core stabilize the torso.

exercises.name: Lever Rear Foot Elevanted Split Squat (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Set the desired load on the lever machine and adjust the rear foot platform/pad so the top of your rear foot can rest comfortably when standing in position. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the desired load on the lever machine and adjust the rear foot platform/pad so the top of your rear foot can rest comfortably when standing in position.

Stand facing forward in front of the machine and place the top of your rear foot onto the elevated lever/platform so your front foot is planted on the floor in a split stance (front foot roughly 2–3 feet in front of the rear platform).
Grip the machine handles or hold the lever for stability, keep your chest up, shoulders back, and brace your abs; maintain an upright torso throughout.

Slowly lower your body by bending the front knee and hip until the front thigh is about parallel to the floor or the front knee reaches ~90° (rear knee approaching but not touching the platform). Ensure the front knee tracks over the midline of the foot and does not collapse inward.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then press through the front heel to extend the knee and hip and return to the starting upright position in a controlled manner.
Repeat for the desired reps, then carefully step forward, switch legs, and perform the same number of reps on the opposite side.
exercises.description: A single‑leg split squat performed with the rear foot elevated on a lever/plate-loaded platform; this variation emphasizes the front leg's knee and hip extension to load the quadriceps while challenging single‑leg stability. The movement is performed standing with the rear foot supported on the machine's elevated pad.

exercises.name: Barbell Hook grip Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and bend knees to grip the bar just outside your legs using a hook grip (thumb wrapped around bar, secured by first two fingers). Stop 1 rep short of failure (RPE 9). Spinal safety takes priority.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, toes pointing forward or slightly outward, with the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and bend knees to grip the bar just outside your legs using a hook grip (thumb wrapped around bar, secured by first two fingers).
Brace your core, pull shoulder blades down and back, and maintain a neutral spine with chest up.
Drive through your heels, extending hips and knees simultaneously while keeping the bar close to your body.
Stand tall at the top with hips fully extended and shoulders back, then lower under control by hinging at the hips first.
exercises.description: A barbell deadlift performed with a hook grip using a conventional stance that emphasizes hip extension and the posterior chain. The hook grip allows for a stronger hold on the bar without relying on mixed grip, while training powerful hip and knee extension with strong back stability.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Decline Push-Up
exercises.notes: Extend your body into a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core and glutes to maintain a rigid plank position throughout the movement.
instructions: Place your feet on a bench or elevated surface and position your hands on the floor slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, fingers pointing forward.
Extend your body into a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core and glutes to maintain a rigid plank position throughout the movement.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows at a 30-45 degree angle from your torso, keeping your head neutral and spine aligned.
Press through your palms to push back up to the starting position, fully extending your arms without locking the elbows.
Maintain core tension throughout and avoid letting your hips sag or pike up during the movement.
exercises.description: A decline push-up performed with feet elevated on a bench, placing greater emphasis on the upper chest and anterior deltoids. The elevated foot position increases the difficulty compared to standard push-ups while targeting the pectorals, with secondary engagement from the triceps, anterior delts, serratus anterior, and core for stabilization.

exercises.name: Diamond Push-up
exercises.notes: Start in a high plank (prone) position on hands and toes with body in a straight line from head to heels; engage the abs and glutes to maintain a rigid torso.
instructions: Start in a high plank (prone) position on hands and toes with body in a straight line from head to heels; engage the abs and glutes to maintain a rigid torso.
Bring your hands together directly under your sternum so thumbs and index fingers touch, forming a diamond/triangle shape with your hands.
Keep your elbows close to your sides (not flared) and your neck neutral; this emphasizes the triceps.
Inhale and slowly lower your chest toward the diamond so your elbows bend to about 90 degrees or until your chest nearly touches your hands.
Pause briefly at the bottom without collapsing the torso, then exhale and press through your palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high‑plank position.
Repeat for desired reps, maintaining controlled tempo and core stability throughout.
Modification: perform the same movement from the knees (kneeling) to reduce load if full high‑plank diamond push‑ups are too difficult.
exercises.description: A close‑hand push‑up variation performed from a high‑plank (prone) position with thumbs and index fingers together forming a diamond to place greater load on the triceps while also engaging the chest and shoulders.

exercises.name: Lying 90 degrees Alternate Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Start lying supine (on your back) with hips and knees both flexed to about 90 degrees (thighs vertical, shins horizontal).
instructions: Start lying supine (on your back) with hips and knees both flexed to about 90 degrees (thighs vertical, shins horizontal). Arms rest by your sides for support.
Brace your core and press your lower back lightly into the floor to prevent arching; keep hips level throughout the set.
Slowly extend one leg by straightening the knee until the leg is nearly straight (do not hyperextend or lock the knee). Maintain the hip at ~90 degrees (thigh stays vertical).
Pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly bend the knee to return that leg to the starting 90-degree position.
Immediately repeat the same extension with the opposite leg, alternating legs in a controlled rhythm.
Keep movement slow and deliberate, exhaling during the extension and inhaling during the return, and avoid letting the pelvis tilt or the lower back arch.
Perform desired reps or time, adjusting range of motion or tempo if you feel knee pain; stop if sharp pain occurs.
exercises.description: Lying supine with hips and knees bent at 90 degrees, extend one leg at a time by straightening the knee and then return to the start. This movement emphasizes knee extension to load the quadriceps while the core and glutes stabilize the pelvis.

exercises.name: Lying Reverse Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Lie supine (face up) on a flat surface with legs extended, arms by your sides for stability, and head resting comfortably.
instructions: Lie supine (face up) on a flat surface with legs extended, arms by your sides for stability, and head resting comfortably.
Brace your core by engaging the abs to stabilize the pelvis and maintain a neutral lower back throughout the movement.
Keeping legs straight or with a slight bend, squeeze your glutes and hamstrings to lift both legs upward through hip extension.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement while maintaining core tension, then slowly lower legs back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for desired repetitions, focusing on smooth controlled movement and avoiding momentum.
exercises.description: A supine hip-extension movement where you lie face up and lift the legs by contracting the glutes and hamstrings while keeping the torso stable; the abs and lower spine act to stabilize the pelvis throughout the movement.

exercises.name: 90 degrees Heel Touch
exercises.notes: Start lying on your back (supine) with hips and knees bent to 90 degrees so your shins are parallel to the floor and feet off the ground; extend your arms overhead with hands reaching back toward your heels.
instructions: Start lying on your back (supine) with hips and knees bent to 90 degrees so your shins are parallel to the floor and feet off the ground; extend your arms overhead with hands reaching back toward your heels.
Draw your belly button toward your spine and press your lower back lightly into the floor to brace your core.
Exhale and curl your torso up, rotating slightly toward the right hip while reaching your right hand down toward your right heel — keep the motion driven by the core, not by pulling on the neck.
Hold the top position briefly (about 1 second) and feel the contraction in your abs.
Inhale and slowly lower your torso back to the starting supine position with control, keeping your hips and knees stable at 90 degrees.
Repeat the same curl and reach toward the left heel with the left hand, alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions.
Keep movements controlled, maintain a neutral neck (do not yank the head forward), and avoid lifting the lower back off the floor.
exercises.description: Lying supine with hips and knees at 90 degrees, this alternating crunch reaches the hands toward the heels to load the rectus abdominis and rotational core. It emphasizes controlled curling and side-to-side reaching while keeping the lower back stable on the floor.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Landmine Shoulder To Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Stand facing the end of the landmine with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent (athletic stance).
instructions: Stand facing the end of the landmine with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent (athletic stance).
Grasp the end of the bar with both hands close together and bring it up to one shoulder (bar resting near the front/side of that shoulder) with elbows tucked slightly in.
Brace your core and keep a neutral spine; rotate your torso slightly toward the side of the loaded shoulder to create a stable pressing platform.
Press the bar diagonally across your body to the opposite shoulder level by extending your arms and rotating your torso toward the receiving side; exhale as you press.
Pause briefly when the bar reaches the opposite shoulder, maintaining control and core tension (do not overarch the lower back).
Slowly reverse the motion and bring the bar back to the starting shoulder under control while inhaling.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, then switch sides so the opposite shoulder becomes the starting position.
Keep movements controlled, avoid using excessive momentum, and maintain a braced core and stable lower body throughout.
exercises.description: Standing landmine press where you drive the bar from one shoulder across the body to the opposite shoulder, emphasizing unilateral shoulder pressing and rotational core stability.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and slightly bend the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a 30–45° angle if mobility requires), keeping a neutral spine and chest up.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and a barbell on the floor in front of you.
Hinge at the hips and slightly bend the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a 30–45° angle if mobility requires), keeping a neutral spine and chest up.
Grip the barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width so elbows can travel out to the sides.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades, maintaining a stable torso throughout the movement.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows outward and up, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/upper abdomen while keeping the elbows high to emphasize the rear delts.
At the top, pause briefly and squeeze the rear delts and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Slowly lower the bar under control back to the start position, fully extending the arms while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation to lift the weight.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row variation that specifically emphasizes the rear delts by rowing with elbows flared out to the sides. Performed from a hip-hinged standing position to target the posterior shoulder and upper back.

exercises.name: Barbell Close Grip Larsen Press
exercises.notes: Set up on a flat bench with a barbell racked at appropriate height; lie supine with your legs extended straight out on the bench (Larsen position) rather than feet on the floor.
instructions: Set up on a flat bench with a barbell racked at appropriate height; lie supine with your legs extended straight out on the bench (Larsen position) rather than feet on the floor.
Grip the bar with a close/narrow grip (hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower), retract your shoulder blades, and unrack the bar to hold it over your chest with arms extended.
Lower the bar under control to your chest, keeping elbows tucked closer to your body, and allow the bar to briefly rest on your chest (dead stop).
Press the bar back up by driving through your chest and triceps until arms are fully extended; maintain core tension throughout since you have no leg drive.
Repeat for desired reps, then carefully re-rack the bar.
exercises.description: A close-grip variation of the bench press performed in the Larsen style with legs extended straight on the bench rather than planted on the floor. This eliminates leg drive and increases core stability demands while the narrow grip emphasizes triceps involvement alongside the pectorals and front delts.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Close Grip Military Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and core braced (standing position).
Grip the barbell with a close/narrow grip (hands slightly narrower than shoulder width) and rest the bar on the front of your shoulders / upper chest in a front-rack position.
Keep wrists neutral and elbows pointing slightly forward, not flared directly out to the sides.
Take a full breath, tighten your abs and upper back, and press the bar upward in a straight path overhead until your arms are fully extended and the bar is roughly over or slightly behind your head.
At the top, lock out the elbows briefly and keep the ribcage down to avoid excessive back arching.
Lower the bar under control back to the starting front-rack position at the collarbone/upper-chest level while inhaling.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a braced core and neutral spine throughout; avoid using excessive leg drive or leaning back.
exercises.description: A standing close-grip variation of the military press where a barbell is pressed overhead from a front-rack position; this emphasizes the delts while increasing triceps involvement due to the narrow grip. Performed standing with a tight core to stabilize the spine.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit at your sides roughly at hip level when seated; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles sit at your sides roughly at hip level when seated; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your back against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and grasp the handles with a neutral grip (palms facing inward).
Keep a slight bend in the elbows and the forearms relatively fixed; start with the arms hanging at your sides.
Exhale and raise your upper arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your elbows reach shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep your torso still and avoid leaning or using momentum; do not excessively shrug the shoulders.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position with controlled movement.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining form throughout.
exercises.description: Seated machine lever lateral raise isolates the lateral delts by abducting the arms out to the sides against resistance, emphasizing controlled movement and minimal torso motion. It targets shoulder width and middle deltoid development.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Pull Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Pull Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Chin-ups (narrow parallel grip)
exercises.notes: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
instructions: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to remove slack before initiating the pull (scapular set).
Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving the elbows toward your hips while focusing on pulling with the lats rather than just the arms.
Continue the pull until your chin clears or reaches the level of the handles and your chest approaches the grips; pause and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1 second.
Inhale and lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full hang with arms fully extended, maintaining shoulder tension and avoiding a sudden drop.
Keep the torso stable and avoid excessive swinging or kipping; if needed, use a controlled bend in the knees or a slight leg cross to prevent momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on smooth tempo, full range of motion, and consistent breathing.
exercises.description: A narrow, neutral-grip chin-up performed from a vertical hang that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the arms and upper back. Pull the body up until the chin clears the handles, then lower under control to a full hang.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Bend your knees slightly and hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable 30–45° incline), keeping a neutral spine and head aligned with your spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell on the floor in front of you.
Bend your knees slightly and hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable 30–45° incline), keeping a neutral spine and head aligned with your spine.
Reach down and grasp the barbell with an underhand (palms facing up) grip about shoulder-width apart; tighten your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly.
Initiate the movement by driving the elbows back and pulling the barbell toward your lower chest/upper abdomen, keeping elbows close to your sides to emphasize the lats.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulder blades squeezed together and the chest slightly elevated, avoiding excessive spine extension or kipping.
Slowly lower the barbell back to the starting hang under control until arms are nearly extended, maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine throughout.
Breathe out as you pull the bar up and inhale as you lower it; keep knees slightly bent and avoid rounding the lower back during the set.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row using an underhand (reverse) grip that emphasizes the lats while also recruiting the biceps and upper back musculature. Performed from a hinged, kneebent standing position and lifting the bar toward the lower chest/upper abdomen.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Wide Grip Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor; keep your spine neutral and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and place a loaded barbell on the floor in front of you.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor; keep your spine neutral and head in line with the spine.
Grip the bar with a wide overhand grip (wider than shoulder-width) and let your arms hang fully extended.
Brace your core, inhale, then pull the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows out and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders retracted, then exhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner until arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired repetitions while avoiding torso rotation, excessive momentum, or spinal rounding; keep knees slightly bent and maintain the hip hinge throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed with a wide overhand grip that emphasizes the upper back by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the bar toward the lower chest/upper abdomen. The torso stays hinged forward while the hips and spine stabilize the movement.

exercises.name: Barbell Pullover To Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
Start with the barbell held vertically over your chest with elbows slightly bent and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.

Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in an arc behind your head by flexing at the shoulders, keeping the upper arms angled and elbows soft until you feel a stretch across the lats/pectorals (do not let the lower back over-arch).
Pause briefly at the bottom of the pullover stretch while maintaining core tension and controlled breathing.
Drive the barbell back in a controlled arc over the chest by contracting the lats and pectorals; as the bar reaches chest level, transition into the press.
Exhale and press the barbell straight up until arms are fully extended overhead, using shoulders and triceps to finish the movement while keeping the ribs down and abs engaged.
Slowly lower the barbell back down to the chest under control to reset for the next rep, maintaining a steady rhythm and avoiding excessive momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Use a manageable weight to protect the shoulders and maintain proper form throughout.
exercises.description: A supine combination movement that begins as a barbell pullover (bringing the weight in an arc behind the head to stretch and work the lats) and finishes as an overhead press, adding shoulder and triceps work. It trains lat length and upper‑body pressing strength while requiring core stabilization.

exercises.name: Lever Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
instructions: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Step 2: Adjust the seat and pivot so the lever handles start just above and behind your head when your arms are extended; select an appropriate weight.
Step 3: Reach up and grasp the handles with a comfortable (typically neutral or slightly pronated) grip, keeping a small, fixed bend in the elbows.
Step 4: Begin with the shoulders stretched overhead and the chest lifted slightly, maintaining a stable torso against the pad.
Step 5: Exhale and pull the lever in a controlled arc down and back toward your hips/upper thighs, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the lats.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement with elbows by your sides and a full lat contraction; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly return the lever to the start position, resisting the weight and keeping tension on the lats without letting the shoulders fully relax.
Step 8: Repeat for desired reps; maintain strict form—no torso rocking, keep core engaged, and avoid locking the elbows at the end ranges.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover that moves the arms in an overhead-to-hip arc to lengthen and contract the lats while also engaging the pectorals and serratus anterior. Performed on a lever machine with the torso supported by a backrest for stability.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up supine on a flat bench with feet planted firmly on the floor and shoulder blades pulled together; maintain a slight natural arch in the lower back and keep glutes on the bench.
instructions: Set up supine on a flat bench with feet planted firmly on the floor and shoulder blades pulled together; maintain a slight natural arch in the lower back and keep glutes on the bench.
Grasp the bar with a wide grip (hands wider than shoulder-width) so the forearms are roughly vertical at the bottom of the movement; wrists should remain neutral.
Unrack the bar with straight arms and position it directly above your shoulders at arm's length before beginning the descent.
Slowly lower the bar in a controlled line toward the mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows moved outward (more flare than a narrow grip) to emphasize the pectorals.
Touch the chest lightly without bouncing; pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the chest and shoulders.
Drive the bar upward and slightly backward to the starting position by extending the elbows and contracting the pectorals; exhale during the press.
Keep the core braced and feet stable throughout each rep; avoid excessive shoulder shrugging or lifting the hips off the bench.
Re-rack the bar under control when finished or when a spotter assists; use a competent spotter for heavy loads and ensure safety clips are used on the bar.
exercises.description: A flat barbell press using a wider-than-shoulder grip that places primary emphasis on the pectorals. Performed lying supine on a bench, the bar is lowered to the chest and pressed back up to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Bench Press on Floor
exercises.notes: Lie flat on the floor with legs extended and grasp the barbell with a reverse (supinated/underhand) grip at shoulder-width.
instructions: Lie flat on the floor with legs extended and grasp the barbell with a reverse (supinated/underhand) grip at shoulder-width.
Unrack or lift the bar to position it directly over your chest with arms fully extended.
Lower the bar slowly until your upper arms touch or nearly touch the floor, keeping elbows tucked at about 45 degrees.
Press the bar back up to full arm extension, squeezing the chest at the top.
Maintain a flat back against the floor throughout the movement and control the tempo on each rep.
exercises.description: A floor-based bench press performed with a reverse (underhand/supinated) grip that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while limiting range of motion. Lying on the floor, you lower the bar to the chest/upper torso and press it back up under control.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down.
Sit with your chest pressed into the pad, grip the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, and keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement.
Start with the arms hanging at your sides (shoulders relaxed) and brace your core to prevent movement of the torso.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (about 90 degrees), keeping the elbows slightly bent and the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the top while avoiding shoulder shrugging (do not lift with the neck or upper trapezius).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position under control, maintaining chest contact with the pad and minimizing momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form without swinging.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported lateral raise performed on a lever machine to isolate the lateral deltoids; the chest pad prevents torso swing so the delts do the work. Arms are raised out to the sides with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Lever Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Step 2: Adjust the seat and pivot so the lever handles start just above and behind your head when your arms are extended; select an appropriate weight.
Step 3: Reach up and grasp the handles with a comfortable (typically neutral or slightly pronated) grip, keeping a small, fixed bend in the elbows.
Step 4: Begin with the shoulders stretched overhead and the chest lifted slightly, maintaining a stable torso against the pad.
Step 5: Exhale and pull the lever in a controlled arc down and back toward your hips/upper thighs, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the lats.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement with elbows by your sides and a full lat contraction; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly return the lever to the start position, resisting the weight and keeping tension on the lats without letting the shoulders fully relax.
Step 8: Repeat for desired reps; maintain strict form—no torso rocking, keep core engaged, and avoid locking the elbows at the end ranges.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover that moves the arms in an overhead-to-hip arc to lengthen and contract the lats while also engaging the pectorals and serratus anterior. Performed on a lever machine with the torso supported by a backrest for stability.

exercises.name: Lever Alternating Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the back pad supports your upper back and the lever handles sit just above/behind your head when seated upright.
Load an appropriate plate weight and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent; brace your core to maintain a neutral spine.
Grasp the right and left lever handles (one in each hand) with a neutral or overhand grip. Begin with both arms extended overhead with a slight bend in the elbows.

For each rep, exhale and pull one lever down and in a smooth arc toward your chest/upper stomach using the shoulder joint — lead with the upper arm, not the wrist, and keep the elbow slightly bent and fixed.
At the bottom of the movement, pause briefly and focus on squeezing the pectorals and lats on the working side while keeping your torso stationary.
Inhale and slowly return the lever under control back to the start (overhead) to complete the eccentric phase; avoid letting the shoulder snap back.
Alternate sides each repetition (right arm then left arm) while maintaining the same tempo, core bracing, and no excessive torso rotation or lumbar arch.
Keep movements controlled, avoid shrugging the shoulders, and reduce weight if you cannot maintain a fixed elbow angle and stable torso throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover is a single-arm/alternating machine pullover that moves the upper arm in an arc from overhead down toward the chest, emphasizing the pectorals while also recruiting the lats and serratus anterior. The movement is performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the machine: choose an appropriate weight, adjust the seat height so the pivot of the lever lines up with your shoulder joint and the handles start near your hips.
Sit upright with your back pressed into the seat pad, feet flat on the floor, and chest up; grasp the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip.
Start with your elbows slightly bent and arms resting at your sides (not locked), maintaining a neutral wrist and steady grip.
Exhale and lift the handles out to your sides in a controlled arc, leading with the elbow so the upper arm reaches about parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Keep your torso still and avoid shrugging your shoulders; use your delts to move the weight rather than momentum or back extension.
Pause briefly at the top with the elbows slightly higher than the wrists, feeling tension in the delts and traps for scapular stability.
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position with control, maintaining the slight elbow bend.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Prioritize controlled tempo, full range without swinging, and maintain scapular stability throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever lateral raise is a machine exercise that isolates the lateral head of the delts by abducting the arms out to the sides from a seated, supported position. It emphasizes controlled movement to raise the arms to about shoulder height while minimizing trunk movement.

exercises.name: Lever Lateral Raise with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your chest rests firmly against the pad and your feet are flat on the floor; the lever handles should be at about hip height when your arms hang down.
Sit with your chest pressed into the pad, grip the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, and keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement.
Start with the arms hanging at your sides (shoulders relaxed) and brace your core to prevent movement of the torso.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (about 90 degrees), keeping the elbows slightly bent and the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the top while avoiding shoulder shrugging (do not lift with the neck or upper trapezius).
Inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position under control, maintaining chest contact with the pad and minimizing momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form without swinging.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported lateral raise performed on a lever machine to isolate the lateral deltoids; the chest pad prevents torso swing so the delts do the work. Arms are raised out to the sides with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the machine weight so you can perform controlled reps without shoulder shrugging.
Stand upright beside the lever with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; grasp the handle with the working hand (thumb wrapped) at your side.
Keep a slight bend in the elbow and the wrist neutral; maintain an upright torso with shoulders down and back (no leaning or twisting).
Exhale and lift the arm straight out to the side in a smooth arc until the upper arm reaches roughly shoulder height (parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the scapula stable and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position under control, resisting any momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides if performing single-arm sets.
exercises.description: A standing lateral raise performed on a lever/cable station that isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting the arm out to the side from the body. The movement uses a controlled upward arc to shoulder height and a slow descent to emphasize the delts.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

workout.title: Full Body - STRENGTH/ENDURANCE
workout.description: Full Body workout focusing on strength and endurance training.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Decline Push-Up
exercises.notes: Extend your body into a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core and glutes to maintain a rigid plank position throughout the movement.
instructions: Place your feet on a bench or elevated surface and position your hands on the floor slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, fingers pointing forward.
Extend your body into a straight line from head to heels, engaging your core and glutes to maintain a rigid plank position throughout the movement.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows at a 30-45 degree angle from your torso, keeping your head neutral and spine aligned.
Press through your palms to push back up to the starting position, fully extending your arms without locking the elbows.
Maintain core tension throughout and avoid letting your hips sag or pike up during the movement.
exercises.description: A decline push-up performed with feet elevated on a bench, placing greater emphasis on the upper chest and anterior deltoids. The elevated foot position increases the difficulty compared to standard push-ups while targeting the pectorals, with secondary engagement from the triceps, anterior delts, serratus anterior, and core for stabilization.

exercises.name: Landmine Shoulders Press with Viking Press Attachment
exercises.notes: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neutral spine, and position the bar angled away from you so it can travel upward without hitting your head.
Inhale, then press the handles upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arms are fully extended overhead or slightly behind the head, avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged and elbows unlocked but not hyperextended.
Exhale as you slowly lower the handles back down to the starting shoulder-height position under control, maintaining tension in the delts and core.
Repeat for the desired reps; keep movement smooth and controlled, avoid leaning the torso excessively back or using lower-body drive to move the load.
Adjust stance or hand position to shift emphasis (wider grip = more lateral delt emphasis; closer grip = more anterior delt/pectorals) and lower the weight if you cannot maintain strict form.
exercises.description: A standing landmine press using a Viking press attachment that emphasizes overhead pressing from the shoulders; the movement drives the handles upward from shoulder height to full arm extension, targeting the delts while the triceps and upper chest assist.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start in a high-plank position with hands placed wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start in a high-plank position with hands placed wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width), fingers splayed, and body forming a straight line from head to heels.
Brace your core and glutes to maintain a rigid body position with neutral spine and neck.
Lower your body by bending the elbows outward until your chest is close to the floor or elbows reach approximately 90 degrees.
Hold this lowered position isometrically, maintaining full-body tension and keeping hips level without sagging or piking.
Breathe steadily while holding for the desired duration, then press back up to complete the set.
exercises.description: An isometric hold variation of the wide hand push-up where you maintain a static lowered position with hands placed wider than shoulder width. This hold maximizes time under tension for the pectorals and core stabilizers while maintaining a rigid body line.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and hold a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip, palms facing away from you.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and hold a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip, palms facing away from you.
Keep your chest up, core braced, and maintain an upright posture throughout the movement.
With arms relatively straight, lift the barbell backward and upward away from your body by squeezing the rear delts.
Raise the bar as high as comfortable while maintaining control, then lower slowly back to the starting position.
Avoid leaning forward or using momentum; focus on controlled movement through the rear deltoids.
exercises.description: A standing barbell exercise performed with the bar held behind the body using an overhand grip. The movement involves lifting the barbell backward and upward away from the glutes by extending the shoulders, keeping arms relatively straight to target the posterior deltoids and traps.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Chin-ups (narrow parallel grip)
exercises.notes: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
instructions: Start by grasping parallel handles overhead with a narrow neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended while hanging from the bar; cross your ankles or keep feet together and maintain a slight hollow body with ribs down.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to remove slack before initiating the pull (scapular set).
Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving the elbows toward your hips while focusing on pulling with the lats rather than just the arms.
Continue the pull until your chin clears or reaches the level of the handles and your chest approaches the grips; pause and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1 second.
Inhale and lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full hang with arms fully extended, maintaining shoulder tension and avoiding a sudden drop.
Keep the torso stable and avoid excessive swinging or kipping; if needed, use a controlled bend in the knees or a slight leg cross to prevent momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on smooth tempo, full range of motion, and consistent breathing.
exercises.description: A narrow, neutral-grip chin-up performed from a vertical hang that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the arms and upper back. Pull the body up until the chin clears the handles, then lower under control to a full hang.

exercises.name: Barbell Paused Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip (or mixed grip if needed for heavy loads) and brace your core.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together and driving your elbows back, bringing the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Hold and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1–2 seconds (the paused portion) while keeping the torso stable and avoiding excess torso rotation.
Lower the bar under control until arms are extended and the bar is near knee level or a natural hang, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for desired reps while breathing out during the pull and inhaling as you lower; avoid rounding the back or using excessive momentum.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed from a hip-hinged position with a brief pause at the top of the pull to maximize scapular retraction and time under tension. Emphasizes rowing the elbows back while keeping the torso stable and the spine neutral.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
instructions: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
Plant feet firmly on the floor, pull your shoulder blades together and down (retract and depress) and maintain a tight core and neutral spine.
Grip the bar with a wide, pronated grip (wider than shoulder-width) so the forearms are more angled when the bar is at chest level.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your chest, keeping shoulders stable and the elbows under the bar.
Inhale, slowly lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest / nipple line while keeping the elbows tracking out at a moderate flare and the scapulae retracted.
Pause briefly with the bar lightly touching the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar back up along a slight arc until the arms are nearly extended.
Avoid locking the elbows aggressively at the top, maintain tension in the pectorals and triceps, and keep the wrists neutral.
Return the bar to the rack with control after your set; use a spotter if lifting heavy or approaching failure.
exercises.description: A barbell bench press performed with a wider-than-shoulder grip to emphasize the outer pectoral fibers. Lying supine on a flat bench, the bar is lowered to the mid-chest and pressed back to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
Lie face down (prone) on the bench so your chest is supported, feet braced on the floor or bench for stability, and your torso is fixed against the pad.
Reach down and grasp the barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip about shoulder-width apart. Allow your arms to hang fully extended.
Before pulling, draw your shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to start the movement from the back rather than the arms.
Pull the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and slightly out.
At the top, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold briefly (about 1 second) while keeping the torso pressed to the bench and neck neutral.
Lower the barbell slowly back to full arm extension under control, maintaining scapular tension and avoiding any bouncing or trunk rotation.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale while lowering. Keep movement strict—do not jerk the weight or lift your chest off the bench.
exercises.description: Prone row performed on an incline bench where the torso is supported and a barbell is pulled toward the lower chest/upper abdomen. It emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the upper back, rear delts and arm muscles.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it securely; position the bench so you can lie prone (face down) with your chest supported and feet braced on the floor or bench frame for stability.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it securely; position the bench so you can lie prone (face down) with your chest supported and feet braced on the floor or bench frame for stability.
Load a barbell with appropriate weight and grasp it with a shoulder-width overhand or neutral grip while lying prone; let your arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Maintain a neutral spine and keep your chest pressed into the bench throughout — avoid lifting or twisting your torso (prone position).
Initiate the rep by retracting your shoulder blades, then pull the barbell upward toward your lower chest / upper abdomen, driving the elbows back and keeping them close to your body.
At the top of the movement, squeeze the lats and upper back for a brief 1–2 second pause while keeping the neck neutral (don't over-rotate the head).
Slowly lower the barbell under control until the arms are nearly extended and the lats are stretched, maintaining scapular control; do not let the shoulders round forward.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale as you lower it. Use controlled tempo and avoid using momentum or jerking the weight.
Adjust grip width or elbow flare slightly to emphasize more upper-back/delts (wider) or lats/biceps (narrower), but always keep the chest supported on the bench to prevent torso movement.
exercises.description: Prone incline barbell row performed lying chest-down on an incline bench, pulling a barbell toward the torso to target the lats and mid/upper back. Keep the chest supported on the bench and use scapular retraction to drive the movement rather than torso momentum.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Incline Bench Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
Load a barbell and grasp it with a shoulder-width reverse (underhand) grip so palms face up and arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Start with your chest pressed into the bench, shoulders relaxed and scapulae slightly protracted, core engaged and neck neutral.

Initiate the movement by retracting your scapulae and pulling the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest / upper abdomen, leading with the elbows and keeping them close to your sides.
Pause 1 second at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back without hyperextending the spine or lifting your chest off the bench.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the start with full arm extension while maintaining tension in the lats; avoid dropping the weight or using momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out while pulling and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: Prone incline bench row performed with a reverse (underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while also engaging the upper back and arms. Lying chest-down on an incline bench, you pull a barbell up toward the lower chest/upper abdomen and lower it under control.

exercises.name: Lever Pullover (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
instructions: Step 1: Sit on the machine with your back pressed firmly against the backrest, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Step 2: Adjust the seat and pivot so the lever handles start just above and behind your head when your arms are extended; select an appropriate weight.
Step 3: Reach up and grasp the handles with a comfortable (typically neutral or slightly pronated) grip, keeping a small, fixed bend in the elbows.
Step 4: Begin with the shoulders stretched overhead and the chest lifted slightly, maintaining a stable torso against the pad.
Step 5: Exhale and pull the lever in a controlled arc down and back toward your hips/upper thighs, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the lats.
Step 6: Pause briefly at the bottom of the movement with elbows by your sides and a full lat contraction; avoid shrugging the shoulders.
Step 7: Inhale and slowly return the lever to the start position, resisting the weight and keeping tension on the lats without letting the shoulders fully relax.
Step 8: Repeat for desired reps; maintain strict form—no torso rocking, keep core engaged, and avoid locking the elbows at the end ranges.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded lever pullover that moves the arms in an overhead-to-hip arc to lengthen and contract the lats while also engaging the pectorals and serratus anterior. Performed on a lever machine with the torso supported by a backrest for stability.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Drive through heels, explosively extend hips and knees while keeping the bar close to your body, shrugging forcefully at full extension.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot, grip just outside knees with chest up and back flat.
Drive through heels, explosively extend hips and knees while keeping the bar close to your body, shrugging forcefully at full extension.
Pull elbows high and rotate them forward to catch the bar in a front rack position on your shoulders.
Stand tall to full hip and knee extension, then press the bar overhead until arms are fully locked out.
Lower the bar back to front rack, then return to the floor with control by hinging at hips and bending knees.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift that combines a barbell clean (explosive hip extension and pull to bring the bar from the floor to the front rack position) with an overhead press. The clean phase trains explosive power through the legs, hips, and posterior chain, while the press phase targets the shoulders. This full-body movement recruits multiple muscle groups for power generation and stability.

exercises.name: Landmine Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Set up the landmine by anchoring one end of the bar in the pivot; sit near the end of a flat bench so the bar can be held at chest level in front of you.
instructions: Set up the landmine by anchoring one end of the bar in the pivot; sit near the end of a flat bench so the bar can be held at chest level in front of you.

Grip the end of the bar with both hands close together (or one hand for single-arm variations) and bring the bar to your upper chest/collarbone area; feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and sit tall with a neutral spine.
Brace your core and keep your chest up and shoulders down/squared (scapulae stable) before initiating the press.
Exhale and press the bar upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arms are mostly extended overhead; avoid hyperextending the elbows or leaning excessively backward.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the delts and triceps, then inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the starting position at your chest under control.
Perform repetitions with steady tempo, maintaining a braced core, neutral spine, and controlled shoulder/scapular position throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated landmine shoulder press performed with the bar anchored in a landmine pivot and pressed upward/forward from the chest; primarily targets the delts while the triceps and upper-back musculature stabilize and assist. The seated position reduces lower-body involvement and emphasizes shoulder drive through an arcing press.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and slightly bend the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a 30–45° angle if mobility requires), keeping a neutral spine and chest up.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and a barbell on the floor in front of you.
Hinge at the hips and slightly bend the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a 30–45° angle if mobility requires), keeping a neutral spine and chest up.
Grip the barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width so elbows can travel out to the sides.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades, maintaining a stable torso throughout the movement.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows outward and up, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/upper abdomen while keeping the elbows high to emphasize the rear delts.
At the top, pause briefly and squeeze the rear delts and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Slowly lower the bar under control back to the start position, fully extending the arms while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation to lift the weight.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row variation that specifically emphasizes the rear delts by rowing with elbows flared out to the sides. Performed from a hip-hinged standing position to target the posterior shoulder and upper back.

exercises.name: Barbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and hold a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip, palms facing away from you.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and hold a barbell behind your body with an overhand grip, palms facing away from you.
Keep your chest up, core braced, and maintain an upright posture throughout the movement.
With arms relatively straight, lift the barbell backward and upward away from your body by squeezing the rear delts.
Raise the bar as high as comfortable while maintaining control, then lower slowly back to the starting position.
Avoid leaning forward or using momentum; focus on controlled movement through the rear deltoids.
exercises.description: A standing barbell exercise performed with the bar held behind the body using an overhand grip. The movement involves lifting the barbell backward and upward away from the glutes by extending the shoulders, keeping arms relatively straight to target the posterior deltoids and traps.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and hold the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip about shoulder-width; let the bar rest against your thighs.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and hold the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip about shoulder-width; let the bar rest against your thighs.
Brace your abs, keep your chest up and spine neutral, and maintain a slight bend in the elbows throughout the movement.
Exhale and raise the barbell straight in front of you by lifting at the shoulders until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height); lead the movement with your elbows.
Hold the top position briefly (0–1 seconds) while keeping the shoulders engaged and avoiding shrugging or excessive leaning back.
Inhale and lower the barbell slowly and under control back to the starting position at your thighs.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, using a weight light enough to avoid swinging or recruiting the legs/back; focus on slow, controlled motion.
exercises.description: A standing barbell front raise that emphasizes the anterior delts by lifting a barbell from the thighs to shoulder height with straight or slightly bent arms while keeping the torso stable to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Lying Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it so you can lie face down (prone) with your chest supported and feet braced on the floor or toes on the floor behind you.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place it so you can lie face down (prone) with your chest supported and feet braced on the floor or toes on the floor behind you.

Grasp a barbell with a shoulder-width (or slightly wider) grip, palms facing down (pronated) or neutral if using a specialty bar, letting the bar hang straight down toward the floor with a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and keep your neck neutral, chest pressed into the bench to prevent using torso momentum.

Exhale and raise the barbell out to your sides in a controlled arc by horizontally abducting the shoulders, leading with the elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or at shoulder level.
Pause briefly at the top and focus on squeezing the rear delts, traps, and upper back; avoid shrugging the shoulders or using excessive momentum.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position under control, maintaining the slight elbow bend and chest contact with the bench.
Perform the desired number of reps with smooth tempo; choose light-to-moderate weight to maintain strict form and protect the shoulder joint.
exercises.description: Prone on an incline bench, lift a barbell out to the sides in a horizontal abduction, emphasizing contraction of the rear delts. The chest is supported on the bench while the arms move in an arc to shoulder level with a slight elbow bend.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
